speaker: interviewer
text: --Maurice Moore. OK, would you tell us what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, when we came back from the Nosarzewo camp, the-- the administration [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told the community that we're going to be liquidated as a ghetto, and they're going to resettle us, but we don't know where. And there were 10,000 Jewish people in that ghetto. And there were going to be five groups, each group consisting for about 2,000 people, that are leaving the ghetto of Plonsk. And while I was in the camps, I met a Polish man who told me that the Jews are being liquidated in Auschwitz and in many other parts in Poland, that they were being gassed and burned. And if I could see myself any way possible to run away or save myself in any other ways, he wanted me to know what's going on. And that's the only thing I knew, that there is a possibility that the Germans actually kill the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew. He was a civilian. So while the transfers were taking place, the list of the transfers were supposed to be the Judenrat and the police, and all other people that deemed themselves privileged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I decided to go with one transport before the last transport, so I went with the fourth transport. And they took us in regular trains, passenger trains, from Plonsk to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were in a passenger, pretty much cramped, but it was a Polish passenger train coming from Plonsk to Auschwitz. And in Auschwitz, we came in in the middle of the night. They told us that the children and the older people and the women are going to go to one side, and they will select men [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in another side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I tell you, I was a small person with a big coat on me, with wool coat, winter time. I just didn't know what's going to happen to me. Are they going to look at me as a child or a man? So I-- I saw two Germans with their bayonets drawn, to separate the groups. I just pushed myself under, and I went over to the men's side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew, all these times, that when I worked, I had a chance to survive. And I figure, when they take men, most likely, the men are going to go to work. And I guessed it right. Because the 1,800 people, the women, the children, the older people-- the older men, they went directly to Birkenau to be gassed and burned. And the 200 people that I was among were going to Auschwitz into the camps, into barrack number 10, and we were waiting for 10 days, and then they came, and they asked for volunteers, and I was one of the volunteers. So they took out 100 men from that group to go to a coal mine in a sub-camp of Auschwitz by the name of Jawischowitza.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went under the bayonets, they let you do that? They let you select yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they couldn't do nothing. What were they going to do? There were 2,000 people they were watching. They're not gonna-- they don't know me. They don't care about who went one way or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was right when you got off the train? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Off the train. And it was pitch dark. They didn't know-- they didn't see my face. So it was a-- just a lucky thing. A gutsy thing to do. That's what I always had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were at this sub-camp of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at a sub-camp, working in a coal mine for 25 months, every single day. And in that camp, the first 10 months was-- I didn't think I'd survive. I was that thin. The work was very hard. We were working with Polish miners. Every one of them was an anti-Semite. He-- they didn't like the Jews for some reason. They said, the Jews are not good workers. The Jews were storekeepers and lawyers, engineers, but they never worked hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- whoever was there learned how to work hard. I was working hard for two camps already, so I knew how to work. I wasn't afraid for work. So because I wasn't afraid to work, eventually, I made a friend, a-- a Polish engineer. I made a friend of him, and he used to bring me sandwiches every day. When? At night, or when you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 Months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: After 10 months, you became friendly with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we had a German engineer, but they changed to a Polish engineer. And it just so happened that this engineer was my height, a short guy. And I speak very eloquent Polish. And I was in his league, speaking Polish, and he liked it. Then he became friendly with me, and he asked me if I read German-- and I said yes, I read German-- if I can translate German into Polish and tell him what's going on. I said I'll be happy to do it. And he used to-- and he changed my job to a real good job. It was-- it was just an easy job. At that time, it was-- they took the Polaks to Germany, because they took the Germans into the army. So the Polish miners, they took to the German mines, and the Jewish miners that were helpers, that had something on the ball, gave-- they gave them better jobs, replacing the Polish miners that went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lucky enough, and I was already well acquainted with the type of work to be done in the mines. So I got an excellent job. Except for, the first two days on the job, I had an accident.

speaker: interviewee
text: A piece of coal hit me right on the nose, broke my teeth. And I had this kind of a face, swollen and bloody. And everything stopped, because I was in a special kind of situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything stopped after your accident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So-- but this particular-- I was very lucky, because this engineer gave me a note that I am an essential worker in the mine, that I have to be back tomorrow. I asked him to give me the paper. Even though I didn't feel good, and I was bloodied up, and I washed myself off, and I broke a tooth, and my nose was swollen, I wanted to come back. The reason I wanted to come back is because, anyone that got in an accident in the mines, they never returned in the mines. Because every 10 days, they used to take-- come-- came a doctor to the camp, and selected about 200 people that looked like they don't have any strength, their muscles became soft, and-- or they were in an accident. And they took them to Birkenau, replaced them with newcomers. Because there were constant people coming, new people coming to the camp. So it wasn't a big thing for the Germans to replace one of these coal miners. And therefore-- and I knew about it. I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to work, and this engineer took care of me. I had an easy job-- an easier job than-- than before. And he brought me papers, newspapers. I translated the German into Polish, and I, also, was aware of what's going on, because I was reading. Otherwise, I couldn't get a paper. So I knew that the German army is pulling back from Russia, and with different kind of excuses. They're building bridgeheads, we're building a bridgehead here, and a bridgehead here, and we're going back because this part was not good, and that part was not good. And all these rivers were frozen. All kind of excuses, but we already knew that they're losing the war. So that was close to 1944. Did you-- were you able to share this information with anyone except for this Polish engineer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was too dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Too dangerous. Did you-- did he ask you to do anything else for him, besides translate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But he was instrumental in your living, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He helped me. I also had a miner that I worked with previous to that that I promised that-- that I come from a rich family in Warsaw. That I-- after, if he is going to help me, bring me some food, that I'll give him part of my inheritance. And he-- I don't know if he believed me or not, but he brought me a sandwich anyways.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that in this-- in this sub-camp, it wasn't all Nazis at all. It was the Polish miners and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish miners.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Polish engineers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Engineers and Jewish prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not everyone you worked with was Anti-Semitic. Some were, but then there were these people who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they weren't friendly, let's put it that way. They were-- you know, miners work very hard. It wasn't a picnic for them, neither. And they didn't have what they really got before the war, as miners. They had a limited amount of food, they had a limited amount of money. They were like semi-prisoners, in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they living there at the camp? Were they living at the camp there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, they were civilians. But their economic condition wasn't very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about actually working in the coal mine. Did you go underground every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the coal mines, we were marching from the camp to the coal mines about three, two and a half miles from the camp to the-- to the mines. In the mines, they had shafts that brought us into the mines about 300, 400 feet, 600 feet, 1,800. There were different kind of levels in the mine that you had to work, digging coal. And the coal-- it was like a city, except it was dark, and everyone had his own lamp, and his lamp is his guide, like a guide dog for a blind person. Without this lamp, you're dead. So, in many cases-- just breaking up the story-- many people committed suicide by throwing away the lamp. And they throw away the lamp, that was it. They got lost in the mine, and they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us the difference between you and the people-- the attitude of you, a young person, and the people who committed suicide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you what. I really wanted to survive. I did everything possible to survive. I had to survive. Actually, some situations couldn't be helped, when you're surrounded, and there's no place to go. You know, like the people that were taken to Birkenau. They had no choice. That's it for them. It was just like cattle. And when you worked with civilians, you had a chance. You can lie, you can talk your way out, you can do all kind of things. You had to work-- had a chance to work with civilians. Otherwise, your chances were nil.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lucky that I was at a work camp. I was healthy, and that really kept me alive. Because a lot of people got sick, and they-- that was it. You got sick, it's all over. They don't need you. Why would they take you to a hospital? There wasn't hospitals. There was no hospital for prisoners. Once you got sick, it's goodbye. You said something to me about the men who had lost their wives and children. It was very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. These are actually the people that didn't want to live any longer. They not only went to the-- in my camp, they went in-- in the mines and threw away the lamps, but they also were through the-- in the electric fences. They jumped into the fence to be electrocuted. Lots of people. And also, the routine, you wanted to know about the routine. I'm going to tell you about it. We had to work eight hours in the coal mine, because there were three shifts. One shift prepares for the next shift. It's a routine that takes place in 24-hour segments. And if you didn't-- if you didn't finish your job in eight hours, and you had, let's see, an hour or two to finish it, you stayed for an extra eight hours, until you get back to the barracks. And you didn't have much sleep to catch up to, because you had to be up in your particular shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a tough life. And then, we had to work in the camps, four hours. So eight hours in the camp-- in-- in the mines, four hours in the camp. An hour to go, an hour to come back. And then, you had to shower every day, because you came out of the camp, you were full of dust. About a half an inch of coal dust on your person and on your clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you had two different types of clothes, one to work in, and one to be in the camp. And, actually, there was a laundry, and you gave the dirty clothes, and you get a set of fresh ones every day. And you showered every day. It was cold water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had a bed, an individual bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Individual?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but in three stories-- three rows up. But you had a bed for yourself. Because they had to give you a pretty good condition, because, otherwise, you wouldn't be able to work. A coal miner, that's a hard work. And they gave you a piece of salami every day. Maybe like-- 10, I would say, maybe, a quarter of a pound of salami, that was-- and soup, and a piece of bread. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of soup? Was it a real soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: A soup-- well, it wasn't-- it wasn't too bad. Soup, you got a good soup. Soup at night. Soup, salami, a piece of bread. And in the morning, you get, like, tea. Some kind of a tea.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all? That's it. Because you got the piece of bread in the evening, and you had to-- had-- you had to divide it. You had to eat half of it, and then keep-- but you-- nobody kept it, because you were very hungry. So you ate everything. So you like, went to work, and you worked without food for eight hours, waiting to get to-- back to the camp to eat. You were-- you got your number in-- at this camp, right? In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: My number?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Exactly when did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Auschwitz-- and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I think it was in November, 1942. Or was it in December?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or December, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. But I was in the end of either November or December. Yeah, probably December. We-- we were taken to that Auschwitz camp, and waiting to be-- the first thing they took us is to go into showers. We had to take everything off, throw away our belongings and our toothbrushes and shoes, and whatever we took with us. Clothing, nothing counted. We had to put it in a pile. We went into the showers. It was lucky that these particular showers didn't contain gas. Because afterwards, we found out-- after we got liberated-- we found out that, some of the showers, people got showered, and instead of water came out gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and we-- we, after the shower, went out the other side, and we got a-- got a towel to dry ourselves. Got a uniform with stripes and some shoes. Now, we had to pick shoes, and I was lucky I got a good pair of shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because with shoes, this was the most important thing in my life, shoes that I could walk. Without being able to walk, you can't work. You can't do anything, because it's snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you wore in the coal mine? Shoes, not boots or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoes, all the time. Good shoes. So I had a Polak who wanted my shoes. And he says, if I don't give him my shoes, he's going to do this, he's going to do that. So I gave him a very-- a very Polish dressing down, because I knew how to speak Polish better than him. And it-- it worked. He walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this another prisoner, or was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Another prisoner, but a Polak. You know, they thought that they are going to be privileged, because the Jews were so downtrodden. Whatever somebody says, it's yes, OK, I'll give it to you. They were afraid. But I knew how to handle him. So I remained with the shoes, and I always made sure that I have a good pair of shoes to work in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to the coal mine, were you just in that uniform, or did they give you something heavy to wear? Was it cold down there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had two-- no, we had a work uniform and a camp uniform, but it's the same fabric, flannel. It was like flannel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that warm enough, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, we-- we were-- we were warm enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the coal went--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't pay attention. We were young. I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the people in the work crews young or just healthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. All kind of ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you know what the coal went towards? What they used the coal for?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they use to--

speaker: interviewer
text: The coal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cold?

speaker: interviewer
text: The coal. The coal that you dug.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the coal. It was regular coal. They used to-- used to make gas, gas out of the coal. They make various kind of chemicals out of coal. They used to use a heating coal, they used to heat their homes. This coal was used in Europe, just like here they use the gas. They used to-- they used to have-- electric-- electric stations used coal for power.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also learn to be a mechanic there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the camps, every-- in-- in the coal mine, everything. Whatever they wanted, I used to do. I learned everything, because of [CHUCKLES] necessity. And I was very young. You're very handy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you happen to leave that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. As-- as the Russian army approached Poland, and the German army somehow got disintegrated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They liquidated the camps around Auschwitz. And in January of 1945, they liquidated Auschwitz and all the sub-camps around Auschwitz. And they took our particular camp on a march, and we were marching about 70 miles to a-- to a train, an open cattle train. I don't know what. It's a coal-- coal train, or a-- it was an open train. And, finally, we reach that train, and the train took us to Buchenwald. And Buchenwald didn't have a direct connection with the station, so we had to go to a city called Weimar, with a W. W-E-I-M-A-R, Weimar, in Thuringia, [INAUDIBLE]. And it took about seven kilometers from Weimar to walk to Buchenwald. And when we reached Buchenwald, in January of 1945, they did not have room for us, and they said we cannot enter the camp. So we had to lodge on the snow outside the gates of Buchenwald. We slept in the snow, hungry, and tired, and cold. And at the next day, half of the prisoners didn't get up. They died in the snow. And we still didn't get any food, and we had to stay another day. And another 50% of them died. Finally, from the 2,000 people that marched out of our camp, maybe 700 survived to enter Buchenwald. And we got to Buchenwald, and we got in a, like, isolation camp. Because they had a regular camp, and they have newcomers, and they didn't want to mix both groups up. So we were what we called the small camp in Buchenwald. And after being there for a couple of weeks, they sent us away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They send us out to a camp by the name of Ohrdruf. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ohrdruf, it was a bad camp. And there were Russians in that camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there were Ukraine. Ukraine. And these guards that guard us were Germans and Ukraines, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the prisoners were also Ukraines.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were members of the Russian army that did not behave in their regular camps were sent to this particular camp called-- they used to call it [NON-ENGLISH] camp. [NON-ENGLISH] camp was a punishable camp, to punish the prisoners that did not behave. And there were really not that much to do there. Did you have trouble from those tough prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: These-- these-- these Ukrainians, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they mistreated these-- they were prisoners-- they mistreated the Jews. They were tall, gigantic people. And when we stayed in line to get our bread ration, they used to grab it. If we didn't take the ration, the bread ration, and put it into our shirts fast enough, they grabbed it out of the hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were a lot of prisoners, didn't eat for days. They died of starvation. Jewish people. And I was lucky, in that particular camp. A German guard that guarded me in the camp, in the coal mining camps, recognized me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a little bit of conversation with me, and he said, hey-- you know, they didn't call you by your name, they called you by your number. And your number was in front of you, you know, on the outfit. And they'll say, I remember you. You were in that coal mine for a long, long, long time, and I think that you should be the one to have a little-- I-- I'm going to give you a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me a job. And I worked part time in the kitchen, and part time I worked at the-- in his office, you know, cleaning and putting away, making fires. Because, well, [CHUCKLES] it's not like here, you switch a button to make fires. You had to gather wood and a little coal to make a fire. They had these stoves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And gave me an opportunity to-- to at least have food, and I didn't have to go outside to work. And I-- I was lucky enough to have enough food not only for myself, but to bring into the barracks where I was staying and to share it with my fellow prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I because some-- some kind of a hero, which made me feel good, not only because I was a hero, but at least I could help somebody. These guys had a problem getting their rations. Not all of them, but many of them. You said there really was no real work to do, for the general population.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no real work, but there was so many German guards, and Ukraine guards, that they actually had to create work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you moved on?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to shovel snow from one place, clean the-- clean the highways. But there was really no specific work.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
